返回本批次

C小书

The Little Book of C

little-book-of.github.io 作者 ghostrss 分数 65 评论 19 2026/03/27 04:35
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 第 1 章 入门
  2. 1. C 是什么以及为什么它仍然很重要 C 是最接近机器的语言，同时仍然感觉写起来很人性化。它不是最新的或最简单的，但它是最强大的之一。每个现代操作系统、编译器和数据库都有一个用 C 编写的核心，从 Linux 和 Git 到 Python 的解释器，甚至浏览器的一部分。学习 C 语言可以给你带来其他语言无法提供的东西：了解计算机的实际工作原理。您将看到如何管理内存、如何移动数据、CPU 如何运行您的代码，以及您编写的所有内容如何变成机器可以理解的微小指令。 C 教导纪律。没有垃圾收集器或安全网。您可以决定何时分配内存、何时释放内存以及忘记时会发生什么。您将学习精确性和控制力，这些技能可以成为任何语言的优秀程序员。
  3. 小代码 #include <stdio.h>
  4. int main(void) { printf("你好，C 世界！\n");返回0；运行这个，你就完成了每个 C 程序员开始做的事情，将你的第一行文本打印到屏幕上。它虽小，但却承载着 C 语言的精神：直接、明确、清晰。
  5. 为什么它很重要 C 是所有系统编程的基础。当你理解它时，高级语言就更有意义了。您将了解编译器为何如此工作、为何会发生内存错误以及性能决策如何影响整个程序。即使您从未编写过生产型 C 代码，它所建立的思维方式、仔细的推理、对细节的关注、对机器的尊重，也会影响您用任何语言编写代码的方式。

Original Summary

  1. Chapter 1. Getting Started
  2. 1. What C Is and Why It Still Matters C is the language that sits closest to the machine while still feeling human to write. It’s not the newest or the easiest, but it’s one of the most powerful. Every modern operating system, compiler, and database has a core written in C, from Linux and Git to Python’s interpreter and even parts of your browser. Learning C gives you something no other language can: an understanding of how computers actually work. You’ll see how memory is managed, how data moves, how the CPU runs your code, and how everything you write turns into tiny instructions that the machine understands. C teaches discipline. There’s no garbage collector or safety net. You decide when to allocate memory, when to free it, and what happens when you forget. You learn precision and control, the same skills that make great programmers in any language.
  3. Tiny Code #include <stdio.h>
  4. int main(void) { printf("Hello, C World!\n"); return 0; } Run this and you’ve done what every C programmer starts with, printing your first line of text to the screen. It’s small, but it carries the spirit of C: direct, explicit, and clear.
  5. Why It Matters C is the foundation of all systems programming. When you understand it, higher-level languages make more sense. You’ll see why compilers work the way they do, why memory errors happen, and how performance decisions ripple through an entire program. Even if you never write production C code, the mindset it builds, careful reasoning, attention to detail, respect for the machine, will shape how you write code in any language.

评论（默认中文）

smusamashah 2026/03/27 06:39
我想知道里面有多少幻觉的错误事实。在我了解到它生成的法学硕士之前，它看起来像是一个很好的资源。 https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45479268
I wonder how many hallucinated wrong facts are in there. It looked like a good resource until I learned its LLM generated. https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45479268

在 HN 中查看
robviren 2026/03/27 06:43
我希望有人能教我如何在 Windows 中添加 C 编译器的路径。我们失去了 90% 的人因为学习 C 语言而安装了 C 语言。感觉如今 godbolt 或在线编译器可能是一个合理的起点。 C 语言很神奇，但与愚蠢地打开地球上任何文本编辑器并编写 HTML 文件相比，早期的学习可能会很痛苦。不提倡更多的 JS 学习，但是入门是很难的。
I wish someone spoon fed me how to add path for C compilers in Windows back in the day. We lose a good 90% of people to installing C from ever learning C. Feel like godbolt or an online compiler might be a reasonable starting place these days. C is amazing but can be so punishing early on compared to stupid opening up any text editor on earth and writing an HTML file. Not advocating for more JS learning but it's hard to beat the getting started on that.
在 HN 中查看
anthk 2026/03/27 07:02
大多数 Windows 用户只使用 Codeblocks C/C++ - 或类似的东西 - 并为他们设置一切。
Most Windows users just used Codeblocks C/C++ -or anything similar- and setup everything for them.
在 HN 中查看
JSR_FDED 2026/03/27 08:19
作者关于 C 可以更好地理解计算机、操作系统和其他语言的观点是正确的。

对我来说，一个突破性的时刻是当我看到我的 C 代码与生成的程序集交错时。寄存器、调用约定、调用操作系统函数……一切都暴露无遗！

The author is right about C leading to better understanding of computers, OSes and other languages.

For me a breakthrough moment was when I saw my C code interleaved with the generated assembly. Registers, calling conventions, calling OS functions…all laid bare!

在 HN 中查看
i_am_proteus 2026/03/27 05:33
对于刚接触 C 的人来说，另一个非常好的在线参考是 Beej 的 C 编程指南： https://beej.us/guide/bgc/

（这里引用了K&R，这是对C的标准第一次引用，因为我有义务进行这样的引用。）

Another very fine online reference for someone new to C is Beej's Guide to C Programming: https://beej.us/guide/bgc/

(Here is a reference to K&R, the standard first reference to C, because I am obligated to make such a reference.)

在 HN 中查看
MomsAVoxell 2026/03/27 06:43
我总是发现，每当我将 Peter Van der Lindens 的《深度 C 秘密：专家 C 编程》一书借给同事时，我就再也拿不回来了。 有一段时间，我有大约 10 份备用副本可以分发作为款待，但现在我只是向大家推荐这个 PDF：

https://progforperf.github.io/Expert_C_Programming.pdf

如果您是一名 C 程序员，无论是新的还是老的，并且还没有读过这本书：停止您正在做的事情并去阅读它！ 太棒了。

I always find, whenever I loan Peter Van der Lindens’ “Deep C Secrets: Expert C Programming” book to a fellow colleague, I never get it back. For a while I had 10 or so spare copies to hand out as treats, but now I just refer everyone to this PDF:

https://progforperf.github.io/Expert_C_Programming.pdf

If you’re a C programmer, old or new, and haven’t encountered this book: Stop What You Are Doing And Go Read It! It’s amazing.

在 HN 中查看
pascahousut 2026/03/27 05:43
K&R 参考也很有用。这是一本关于小语言的小书，它没有很多功能，映射到硬件上非常基本的概念，实际上只做非常基本的事情。
And the K&R reference is useful too. It's a small book about a small language that does not have many features and maps to very basic concepts on hardware that really only does very basic things.

melonFella 2026/03/27 06:11

It's so cool! Do you have a similiar resource about c++?

fsckboy 2026/03/27 06:25
我想知道为什么之前的提交被“标记”？

在 HN 中查看
tolerance 2026/03/27 06:38
5 个月前的 HN 显然比现在更不接受涉及法学硕士的任何内容。
The HN of 5 months ago was apparently less receptive to anything made involving LLMs than they are today.
在 HN 中查看
tom_ 2026/03/27 09:02
另一种选择可能是第N次通过的LLM输出不如（N+5个月）第次通过的LLM输出。在所涉及的工作量达到自己完成所需的工作量之前的某个时刻，输出将达到可接受的质量水平……或者您希望如此，如果这项业务有任何意义的话。
Another option might be that Nth pass LLM output is not as good as (N+5 months)th pass LLM output. At some point before the amount of effort involved reaches that required to do it oneself, the output will reach an acceptable quality level... or so you'd hope, if any of this business is to make any sense.
在 HN 中查看
tolerance 2026/03/27 10:56
我想我明白你的意思了，但是这个项目似乎自 2025 年 10 月以来就没有更新过。
I think I follow where you’re coming from but, it doesn’t seem that this project has been updated since October 2025.

androiddrew 2026/03/27 06:33

在 HN 中查看
agrishin 2026/03/27 07:12
它是由人工智能生成的，这一事实既令人兴奋又令人恐惧。 特别是考虑到一些人工智能代理可能接受过这方面的培训。
The fact that it's AI generated is simultaneously thrilling and frightening. Especially considering that some AI Agents might be trained on that.
在 HN 中查看
threethirtytwo 2026/03/27 05:45
艾真的越来越好了。我再也分辨不出其中的区别了。
Ai is getting really good. I can’t tell the difference anymore.
在 HN 中查看
watashiato 2026/03/27 07:04
我可以（这里很明显）但希望我不能。每当我遇到一些我可能想读的东西，但在我已经投入了一些时间之后发现它是LLM“辅助”写作时，感觉就像我被骗吃纸板一样。

当我提出应该明确标记这一点时，最好是在前面，这通常被视为对我个人的怠慢。

我意识到这在某种程度上是我的问题，我不应该对此有强烈的感觉，但我确实如此。

I can (it's really obvious here) and wish I couldn't. Every time I run into something I might wanna read, but it turns out to be LLM "assisted" writing after I've already invested some time, it feels like I was tricked into eating cardboard.

And when I bring up that this should be clearly marked, preferably up front, it's often taken as a personal slight.

I realize this is a me problem to some extend, I shouldn't feel strongly about this, but I do.

在 HN 中查看
girvo 2026/03/27 06:10
有一些非常小的线索，比如人工智能喜欢遵循的恒定的“三规则”，但你是对的，这比以前更难说。
There are some very small tells, like the constant "rule of threes" that AI loves to follow, but you're right that this is much harder to tell than it used to be.
在 HN 中查看
